title: Media Buying Agencies

narrative.executive_view: Media Buying Agencies have an unusually clear AI efficiency story but a fragile lower-middle-market terminal-demand story. NAICS 541830 is specifically the purchase of advertising time or space from media outlets for resale [S1], not creative advertising or selling-side representation. Platform automation and programmatic execution make execution cheaper, yet those same tools shift bargaining power toward platforms and in-house teams. This is a conditional, diligence-heavy acquisition thesis rather than a straightforward AI roll-up.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: Planning, audience research, trafficking, pacing, optimization, reconciliation and reporting are increasingly automated. The IAB Europe survey reports that agencies bought more than half of display budgets programmatically in 2024, with the agency figure at 68% [S2]. IAB's US revenue report also describes AI moving from automation toward models that make autonomous campaign decisions and manage campaigns end to end [S3]. Senior negotiation, cross-platform measurement, vendor relationships and client strategy remain important but are a smaller operational moat than routine execution.
narrative.value_capture: Commission or percentage-of-spend pricing can preserve labor savings better than an hourly-service model, but it also exposes the operator to fee-audit, transparency and self-service pressure. ANA's K2 study found pervasive non-transparent practices and cash rebates across the US media-buying ecosystem [S4]; that supports the need to normalize profit and not treat opaque principal economics as durable savings capture. The selected distribution fraction credits initial margin retention, not unbounded mark-up economics.
narrative.adoption_timing: The 68% agency-side display-programmatic figure exceeds the scorecard threshold, so t50 is zero under the frozen rule [S2]. It is a European, display-focused automation proxy rather than a US NAICS-541830 AI census, and programmatic buying predates generative AI. Still, it is strong evidence that automated execution is already operationally normal enough that a later 50% adoption date would be misleading.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The target universe is thin in the supplied data, and large independents can dominate the statistics. Marketing Dive reports Empower and Ocean Media merged into a $1.5B-billings independent group, combining resources and buying power while maintaining operations [S5]. That demonstrates strategic scale benefits but is not a comp for a $1-10M EBITDA target. Commercial valuation guidance explicitly values media buyers on EBITDA on fees/net revenue rather than gross media spend [S6], making revenue-quality diligence non-negotiable.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is pressured rather than melting. Brands still need cross-platform allocation, attribution, data governance, negotiation and accountable advice, and US internet ad revenue reached $258.6B in 2024 [S3]. But the independent operator's historic execution layer is being automated by programmatic systems and platforms. The likely survivor is a smaller, data-capable advisory/buying specialist; a generalist whose offer is merely placing media is vulnerable to direct platform tools and fee compression.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The supplied 4-digit 5418 occupation mix contains obvious PR, design, promoter and display-role contamination, so the role estimate is explicitly adjusted and carries a large residual. The programmatic statistic is European and measures automation, not US firm-level AI adoption. The multiple source is commercial guidance rather than a disclosed transaction database, and reported receipts can include media pass-through. Diligence must reconcile gross billings to fee revenue, disclose principal/rebate economics, test client concentration and establish whether vendor relationships transfer.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS United States Manual
sources.what_it_supports: Official 541830 scope and exclusions relative to advertising agencies and media representatives.

sources.title: IAB Europe Attitudes to Programmatic Advertising Report 2024
sources.what_it_supports: Agency-side display-programmatic use and automation context; explicitly a Europe-wide industry proxy.

sources.title: Digital Ad Revenue Surges 15% YoY in 2024
sources.what_it_supports: US digital-ad revenue and IAB discussion of AI taking operational campaign tasks.

sources.title: Media Transparency Initiative: K2 Report
sources.what_it_supports: ANA findings on pervasive non-transparent practices and rebates in US media ad buying.

sources.title: Inside the Empower-Ocean merger that formed a $1.5B indie media agency
sources.what_it_supports: Example of independent media-agency consolidation, combined billings, resource sharing and buying-power rationale.

sources.title: Media Buying Agency Valuation
sources.what_it_supports: Commercial media-buying valuation guidance, net-fee EBITDA basis, revenue-recognition risk and 3-8x indicative EBITDA ranges.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Programmatic execution is already widespread and IAB describes AI taking campaign-management tasks; senior strategy and negotiation remain partially human. The 4-digit role proxy requires a large residual judgment.
caveats: Role mix is 4-digit 5418, not pure 541830, and includes obvious non-media-buying occupations.
The programmatic statistic is Europe-wide and not a direct US firm AI census.

role_judgments.rationale: Planning, RFP and buyer workflow automate; vendor and client negotiation remains.
Likely PR proxy contamination, treated conservatively.
Research, optimization and reporting are heavily automated.
Management workflow automates more than commercial accountability.
Likely creative-proxy contamination, assigned limited automation.
Status work and proposals automate; account relationships remain.
Planning support automates but senior client strategy persists.
Likely integrated-agency contamination, assigned limited automation.
Unlisted trafficking, reconciliation, reporting, billing and analytics roles are substantially automatable but not fully eliminated.

pi_dist.rationale: Fee/commission economics can retain labor savings initially, but transparency, rebate and fee-revenue normalization risks limit retention.
caveats: Opaque principal economics and client auditing can materially lower realized distribution retention.

pi_moat.rationale: Scale, vendor relationships, data and attribution can protect margins, but a smaller independent lacks the buying clout of large consolidated firms.
caveats: The consolidation example is vastly larger than lower-middle-market targets.

t50_years.rationale: The 68% agency-side display-programmatic measure exceeds 50%, requiring zero under the frozen threshold rule.
caveats: This is a Europe-wide display automation proxy, not a US NAICS-specific AI adoption measure.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: IAB Europe reports 68% agency-side display budgets purchased programmatically in 2024; it is used as the effective automation baseline.
caveats: The observed measure is European and display-specific; it is not a US 541830 establishment statistic or a pure generative-AI measure.

historical_analogs.value: Programmatic buying already shifted execution from manual placement to software-mediated buying; generative and agentic AI extend automation from execution into campaign decisions and management.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources directly describe programmatic agency use and AI moving into campaign operations.
caveats: Analogy does not predict target-level revenue retention.

selection.rationale: The consolidation evidence supports a fragmented-independent landscape but does not measure owner age; selected value is a cautious operating assumption.
caveats: No NAICS-specific owner-age study was found in reopened sources.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source documents a NAICS-specific succession shortage.
caveats: This is not proof that no individual target has a succession issue.

active_consolidators.rationale: The Empower-Ocean transaction and named Horizon benchmark demonstrate at least several active scaled independent-media buyers; count is conservative rather than a market census.
caveats: Large-scale buyer activity does not prove appetite for every small target.

buy_mult.rationale: A lower-middle-market fee-EBITDA purchase uses the bottom of the guide's 4.5-6x well-run range to reflect transparency and client-concentration risk.
caveats: S6 is commercial guidance rather than a disclosed transaction database; EBITDA must be on net fee revenue, not gross billings.

exit_mult.rationale: A 0.5x improvement assumes documented client retention, transferable vendor economics and data capability, not generic multiple expansion.
caveats: Exit value depends on normalization of pass-through and principal/rebate economics.

pricing_structure.value: Management fees, commissions or percentage-of-spend arrangements; all underwriting should normalize gross billings to net fee revenue.
pricing_structure.rationale: Official scope is buying/resale and the valuation guide states buyers value net fee revenue rather than pass-through media spend.
caveats: Reported receipts and EBITDA can be distorted by gross pass-through and rebates.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Automation is already broad, creating immediate operating-efficiency availability; the score does not treat that as evidence that fees or dollars managed will endure.

terminal_value.justification: Cross-platform advisory remains paid, but programmatic and AI automate core execution while platforms and transparency pressure erode the generalist intermediary position [S2][S3][S4].

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No; the role mix is 4-digit 5418 and the adoption measure is a Europe-wide programmatic proxy, both disclosed.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Commission/fee structures can retain cost savings initially, but transparency reviews, fee audits, self-serve platforms and gross-to-net normalization create deflation and margin-reversal risk.

dataset_mismatch.description: Supplied 4-digit 5418 occupations include PR, creative, promoter and display roles that are not a pure media-buying workforce.
sensitivity: The residual role fraction materially affects V.
Media pass-through can distort the deterministic revenue-based labor-share interpretation, though it is not changed here.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Official 541830 scope is purchase and resale of media time/space; creative agencies and selling-side representatives are explicitly excluded [S1].

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0 under the programmatic proxy threshold; US firm-level AI adoption is not directly observed
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A remains 10 at zero; a stricter AI-only interpretation would lower A.
Moves V materially because the residual is large.
Moves M; gross-billing multiples are invalid for the target.

reviewer_note: Initial fleet packet: all cited score-driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21; valuation and adoption proxies are explicitly disclosed.
